title: 09. Deployment controls
tagline: Take the pager: resolve three production incidents with cache, queue, and rate limit.
description: The operational controls that keep AI apps fast, affordable, and alive: caching, queues, rate limits — and the monitoring that tells you which one you need.

exercises.title: A model call is not a product
exercises.content:
# A model call is not a product

Your demo works. Then real traffic arrives, and three physics problems show up at once: model calls are **slow** (seconds, not milliseconds), **expensive** (per token, forever), and **flaky** (rate limits, timeouts, provider incidents). Production AI engineering is mostly managing those three.

Four controls handle the vast majority of it:

### 📦 Cache — for repeated work
Thousands of users ask *"how do I export my resume?"* — compute the answer once, serve it from cache. Exact-match caching is trivial; semantic caching (embeddings again!) catches paraphrases. Cache hits cost ~nothing and return instantly. **Signature symptom: cost scales with traffic while the distinct-question count doesn't.**

### ⏳ Queue — for slow work
Anything slower than a user's patience (video analysis, bulk processing, long agent runs) must leave the request/response cycle: accept the job, return an id, notify on completion. Retries become safe, spikes flatten into steady worker throughput. **Signature symptom: timeouts and users hammering refresh.**

### 🚦 Rate limit — for hostile or buggy traffic
One scripted client at 500 requests/minute can degrade everyone. Per-user caps contain the blast radius; honest users never notice the ceiling exists. **Signature symptom: one API key dominating your logs.**

### 📊 Monitor — to know which one you need
Latency percentiles (p95, not average), cost per user per day, error rates by type, token counts per request. The controls above are cures; monitoring is the diagnosis. Alert on *trends* — cost per user creeping 10% per week is a fire in slow motion.

### Two more habits of teams that sleep at night
- **Graceful degradation** — when the frontier model times out, fall back to a faster model or a cached answer. Never show a spinner that ends in nothing.
- **Budget alarms** — a hard monthly cap plus a soft alert at 60%. Every AI team has a surprise-bill story; the good ones only have it once.

Next: the pager goes off three times. Diagnose each incident and flip the right switch.
exercises.attribution: Adapted from Claude Quickstarts (MIT), the Gemini Cookbook (Apache-2.0), and "AI Agents for Beginners" by Microsoft (MIT)

exercises.title: Video: AI agents in production
exercises.content:
### Watch: AI agents in production

From Microsoft's *AI Agents for Beginners*. Watch for:

- What they monitor on live agent systems (and what they alert on).
- How cost controls change agent design.
- Their approach to fallbacks when a model call fails.

exercises.title: Game: The control room
exercises.content:
# The control room

You're on call. Three incidents will hit your AI app — a cost spike, a timeout storm, and one very enthusiastic API key. The latency / cost / error gauges show the damage live.

Read each alert, find the **root cause**, and flip the control that fixes it. Wrong switches explain exactly why they don't help — those explanations are interview gold.

*To complete this lesson: resolve all three incidents.*

exercises.title: Knowledge check: ops instincts
exercises.content:
# Knowledge check

Three questions. All three to pass.

quiz.prompt: Why monitor p95 latency instead of average latency?
options: Averages hide the tail — one slow call in twenty is thousands of users having a bad day, invisible in the mean
quiz.explanation: A 2s average with a 30s p95 means 5% of requests are agonizing. Users remember the tail, not the mean.

quiz.prompt: The frontier model API is having an incident. What does a well-built AI app do?
options: Show an error page until the provider recovers
Degrade gracefully — fall back to a faster model or cached answers, and tell users if quality is reduced
Retry the same call in a tight loop
quiz.explanation: Fallback chains keep the product alive through provider incidents. Tight retry loops make their incident your outage.

quiz.prompt: Cost per user is creeping up 10% weekly with no traffic growth. What's your first move?
options: Wait for the monthly bill to confirm the trend
Break down cost by feature and prompt — find what's growing (prompt bloat? longer outputs? retry storms?) before it compounds
Switch every feature to the cheapest model today
quiz.explanation: Diagnosis before medicine: the breakdown tells you whether the fix is a cache, a prompt trim, or a retry bug. Compounding 10% weekly doubles in 8 weeks.

exercises.title: Code lab: Choose a deployment control
exercises.content:
# Choose a deployment control

You diagnosed incidents in the control room — now encode the triage logic. This mapping from symptom to control is a real interview question.

### Your task
Implement `chooseControl(symptom)` and return `cache`, `queue`, `rate-limit`, or `monitor`.

### Source bench
- Claude Quickstarts
- Gemini Cookbook
- mlabonne LLM Course

input: one user sends 500 requests per minute

exercises.hint: Repeated work -> cache. Slow background work -> queue. Too many requests -> rate-limit.
